items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.correct: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.
items.random_wrong: Encrypting data so it cannot be read.
items.why: Students take 'capsule' too literally. Encapsulation combines two ideas: keeping data and behaviour TOGETHER, and HIDING implementation behind a public interface.

items.q: Dijkstra's algorithm fails when edges have...
items.why: It's Negative weights. “Equal weights” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does the 'h' in A*'s f(n) = g(n) + h(n) represent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Heuristic estimate to goal. “History cost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many queens are placed in the classic N-queens problem when N = 8?
items.why: The real figure is 8 queens. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
